item.description: New changes will make it easier to gather evidence, government says

item.title: China vows support for Belarus' national sovereignty
item.description: BEIJING, June 29 (Reuters) - China supports Belarus in safeguarding its national sovereignty, independence and territorial integrity, Chinese President Xi Jinping told Belarusian President Alexander

item.title: Scotland's papers: Scotland squad fly home and Scottish Labour indy review
item.description: A review of the front page stories from the daily newspapers in Scotland.

item.title: Europe Today: Trump's tariff threat, Iran talks and Europe's heatwave
item.description: The US and Iran are set to resume talks in Qatar after a tense weekend of renewed threats, while Europe battles a deadly heatwave linked to more than 1,300 deaths. Attention also turns to growing EU-US trade tensions and the debate over how Europe should adapt to extreme temperatures.

item.title: Russian high schools now require 17 hours of basic military training classes for 6th to 11th grade
item.description: Russia is doubling the amount of military training time in high schools, with new lessons on drones and field exercises.

item.title: Argentine soccer player loses wife, 2 children in Venezuela earthquakes
item.description: An Argentine soccer player for a Venezuelan team lost his wife and two children in last week’s deadly earthquakes.

item.title: Argentine soccer player loses wife, 2 children in Venezuela earthquakes
item.description: An Argentine soccer player for a Venezuelan team lost his wife and two children in last week’s deadly earthquakes.

item.title: Russia rallies support for army with 'patriotic' tourist routes
item.description: Seeing a whole class of children pile into his museum about a Russian prince's 13th-century victory over Western crusaders, historian Vladimir Potresov was delighted."Our museum is small, but recently we've been welcoming around 5,500 visitors a year," said 80-year-old Potresov, who moved from Moscow to the village of Samolva more than a decade ago to found the museum.

item.title: Cape Verde, Africa's outlier in LGBTQ tolerance
item.description: Cape Verdean make-up artist Leonardo put the finishing touches to a singer's look at a shoot for a music video, open and comfortable about being a gay man in Africa."I realised that if I am a gay man who dresses like a man, it's easier to get a job," she added.

item.title: Delcy Rodríguez’s Venezuela is in such dire straits she can’t afford to reject aid from either friends or foes
item.description: Venezuela’s acting President Delcy Rodríguez has little room to turn away any government willing to lend a hand in the aftermath of devastating earthquakes.

item.title: Herzog warns of 'alarming' surge of antisemitism at Romanian pogrom memorial ceremony
item.description: In his speech, Herzog made a connection between the antisemitism of the Holocaust era and the antisemitism Jews experience in the present day. President Isaac Herzog attended a Romanian state ceremony on Sunday marking the 85th anniversary of the Iași pogrom, where he delivered a speech warning of the dangers of antisemitism in the present day.

item.title: Congo says number of confirmed Ebola cases at 1,274, including 360 deaths
item.description: June 29 () - ‌The ‌Democratic Republic ​of Congo said ‌late ⁠on Sunday ⁠that ​confirmed ​Ebola ​cases ‌in the country ‌had ​reached ​1,274, ​including ‌360 deaths. (Reporting ​by ​Sfundo ​Parakozov; ‌Editing by ​Tom ​Hogue).

item.title: China imposes export controls on 40 Japanese entities as tensions with Tokyo rise
item.description: China imposed new export controls Monday on 40 Japanese entities it says are contributing to the country’s “remilitarization,” as tensions with Tokyo rise. Relations between Beijing and Tokyo have been increasingly tense since Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi last year implied Japan could intervene if China used military force against Taiwan, an island democracy China claims as its own.

item.title: Putin admits oil deficit and adjusted fuel plans amid Ukrainian strikes on refineries
item.description: A fresh overnight Ukrainian assault set southern Russia’s major Sloviansk refinery on fire, killing one person.

item.title: Europe's record heat has overwhelmed Paris mortuaries and left families in distress
item.description: Since a record-smashing heat wave started taking lives and storage space for bodies in Paris and beyond, the funeral directors and mourning families calling him mostly have the same question: Do you have room for one more? With all 32 places in his cold room taken, Zouhaeir Hertelli reluctantly has to gently say “Non,” over and over and over again. As the historic heat wave shifted its deadly temperatures eastward this weekend to other parts of Europe, France began counting the human cost it left in its wake.

item.title: Iran cyberattacks on Israel surged in 2026, Israeli cyber chief says
item.description: June 29 (Reuters) - The number of Iranian cyberattacks against Israel has shot up since the launch of the U.S.-Israeli offensive against Iran this year, a senior Israeli security official was quoted

item.title: Pakistan launches ground operation along Afghan border, killing 29
item.description: Pakistani forces launched a ground operation along the Pakistan-Afghan border on Sunday, killing 29 fighters of jihadist groups.

item.title: Pakistan launches deadly strikes along Afghan border
item.description: Pakistan says its strikes were a response to "recent terrorist attacks against innocent people".

item.title: Hospital unveils memorial for baby loss families
item.description: The tribute was inspired by midwife Afton Cope, who lost her son Charlie in 2023.

item.title: Cameroonian baby ape was shot, lost an arm, and still thrived after hunters killed his mother
item.description: "The damage to his arm was so severe that years later, it had to be amputated to relieve the chronic pain he was living with."

item.title: Motorbike shop owner in Malaysia spots python in cracked ceiling — firefighters pull out another surprise
item.description: "I'm afraid there might be snake eggs or babies."

item.title: Two dead, one injured in Austrian river accident
item.description: A 10-year-old boy and a 22-year-old man died in a swimming accident in a river in western Austria on Sunday, police said. The boy's 40-year-old father was seriously injured and taken to hospital.

item.title: Death toll from Venezuela quakes reaches 1,450
item.description: The death toll from twin earthquakes in Venezuela has risen to at least 1,450 with more than 3,200 people injured, authorities said on Sunday. Thousands are still believed to be trapped beneath the rubble, according to National Assembly President Jorge Rodríguez.

item.title: Australia to double potential fines for Facebook and Instagram over child social media accounts
item.description: Australia plans to double potential fines for social media platforms, including Facebook and Instagram, who fail to prevent Australian children from holding accounts as critics argue the world-first ban on under-16s was failing. Communications Minister Anika Wells on Monday blamed the platforms’ resistance to the age restrictions for the need to toughen the laws that came into force on Dec. 10. “We can all agree we would like the scheme to work better than it is currently, but that is on Big Tech taking the Mickey,” Wells told the Australian Broadcasting Corp., using an Australian slang term for deceiving, teasing or mocking.

item.title: Hantavirus outbreak nearing its end, WHO chief says
item.description: The hantavirus outbreak is nearing its end, the head of the World Health Organization (WHO) said on Sunday. WHO Director General Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus said all contacts of two cases identified in South Africa had now completed their follow-up period, with no additional cases reported.

item.title: For sale on Facebook: monkeys, rhino horn and dead pangolins
item.description: The ghostly white creature curled up on a weighing scale is almost unrecognisable in the Facebook post offering it for sale.Some of the content is oblique -- vendors often post images of animals or parts for sale without any price or explanation.

item.title: Ukraine hits 2 Russian refineries as Putin vows enhanced defenses
item.description: Ukraine said it struck a pair of Russian oil refineries far behind the front lines on Sunday as President Vladimir Putin vowed to ramp up defenses.

item.title: Israelis, Palestinians torn over sacred shrine in city of Hebron
item.description: For Israeli settler Nitzan, Hebron's Old City and its sacred Cave of the Patriarchs shrine are a must-see for all of humanity, but for Palestinian Issa Amro, it has become a symbol of Israel's expanding grip on the city.Years later in 1997, Hebron became the territory's only Palestinian city to have an area under direct Israeli military control, named H2, which includes the Cave of the Patriarchs.

item.title: In Sudan's Kordofan, a key city reels as paramilitary offensive looms
item.description: In a displacement camp near El-Obeid in Sudan's southern Kordofan region, Agsam Hamad trudges through searing heat to fetch murky water from a distant well, as paramilitary forces unleash their fiercest assault yet on the strategic city."We walk long distances for this water and it is undrinkable," the 35-year-old mother of seven told AFP from the camp on the edge of El-Obeid, a key prize in the three-year war between the army and the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (RSF).

item.title: Ontario teen says he’ll live in ‘modular home’ invention for a year to prove it can end homelessness
item.description: An 18-year-old Canadian engineering student built a fiberglass modular home to fight homelessness and plans to live in it for a year.

item.title: Woman Rescued with Her 5 Children After Allegedly Being Held Captive by Abusive Husband for More Than a Decade
item.description: Sylvie Yasmina, 54, and her children are now staying in a women's shelter after authorities raided their home in Pakistan

item.title: Pakistan says its security forces killed 29 fighters along Afghan border
item.description: Strikes come a day after fighters armed with guns and explosives killed three soldiers in Karachi.

item.title: Facing China, one Taiwan Coast Guard officer draws strength from the gods
item.description: PENGHU, Taiwan, June 29 (Reuters) - When Yeh Chih-sheng steps aboard Taiwan Coast Guard vessel CG1005 in the choppy waters of the Taiwan Strait, he brings ‌more than his uniform and orders: he also has temple charms blessed by the gods revered by ‌fishermen for protection at sea. Yeh is first mate of the 2,400-ton ship based in Taiwan's Penghu islands, and part of Taipei's maritime frontline as China ​asserts its claim of sovereignty over the democratically governed island.

item.title: A rights group warns Vietnam is ramping up arrests under broad laws to crush dissent
item.description: Vietnam is increasingly using broadly written laws to arrest activists, dissidents and others that authorities consider a threat to the Communist Party's rule, according to a new analysis released Monday by a human rights group. The 88 Project, which focuses on rights issues in Vietnam, documented 56 such arrests in 2025, the third consecutive year of increases and double the number in 2022. The report includes only arrests where the defendant could be identified by name and the case tracked, and the actual numbers are believed to be much higher, said Ben Swanton, co-director of the group.

item.title: Zelensky proposes national pantheon for Ukraine's heroes
item.description: Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky has submitted a bill to parliament to establish a national pantheon honouring the country's heroes. "Today, I submitted to parliament a law on the Ukrainian National Pantheon," Zelensky said on Sunday in an address marking Constitution Day.

item.title: Man due in court charged with attempted murder after car hit pedestrians
item.description: The man has been remanded in custody following a collision in Ealing, west London, on Saturday.

item.title: Pregnant Woman Gives Birth Under the Rubble of Collapsed Buildings After Deadly Earthquakes Hit Venezuela
item.description: An earthquake with a magnitude of 7.1 was followed just seconds later by a 7.5-magnitude tremor on Wednesday, June 24

item.title: Australian man arrested after Thai teen girl found dead in suitcase
item.description: The family of a 17-year-old Thai girl whose body was found in a suitcase in Pattaya said they were devastated by her death, for which an Australian man has been arrested and charged with murder.

item.title: ‘If I am to die, let it be here’: Malawians fleeing unrest in South Africa
item.description: A mass return of Malawians from South Africa exposes the cost of migration, violence and broken livelihoods.

item.title: Pakistan says it struck militant targets in Afghanistan
item.description: Pakistan has again attacked targets in neighbouring Afghanistan, saying it struck hideouts used by militants including the Pakistani Taliban. Information Minister Attaullah Tarar said late on Sunday on X that security forces had carried out a ground operation along the Pakistan-Afghanistan border.

item.title: Venezuela opposition leader Machado plans return home
item.description: Venezuelan opposition leader María Corina Machado plans to return to the country following devastating twin earthquakes. The exiled leader and Nobel Peace Prize laureate told US broadcaster Fox News on Sunday "the time has come" for her to return home.

item.title: Dramatic Video Shows The Moment Rescue Crews Carefully Pull A Baby From the Rubble in Venezuela
item.description: Rescue workers in Venezuela carefully pulled several more people from the rubble — including a small baby — some four days after two earthquakes struck.

item.title: Putin Adopts Nazi Strategy With Strikes On Ukraine’s Most Sacred Sites
item.description: With airstrikes on Ukraine’s cathedrals and temples, and targeted killings of religious leaders, Russia’s Vladimir Putin is reviving the war strategies of Adolph Hitler.

item.title: Putin says Russia will press on with front-line campaign regardless of Ukraine proposals
item.description: June 28 (Reuters) - President Vladimir Putin said on Sunday that Russia will press ahead with its battlefield aim of fully capturing four Ukrainian regions, rejecting what he said was a new proposal by Ukraine to rein in hostilities in the more ‌than four-year-old war. Putin, speaking to a Russian state television interviewer, also said Russia needed to boost its air defence capacity to counter intensified ‌Ukrainian drone attacks aimed mainly at its oil industry.

item.title: At least 15 die in swimming accidents during German heatwave
item.description: At least 15 people have died in swimming accidents across Germany since Friday, according to police reports, as a heatwave sent many people to lakes, rivers and outdoor pools to cool off. Among the latest deaths, a 17-year-old was recovered from a quarry lake in Peine in the northern state of Lower Saxony on Sunday.

item.title: Loved and loathed: The making of India's viral liver doctor
item.description: Dr Cyriac Abby Philips has built a large online following, but often clashes with India's traditional medicine specialists.

item.title: Israel says Hezbollah tunnel destroyed in southern Lebanon
item.description: The Israeli military has destroyed a large underground Hezbollah tunnel in southern Lebanon, Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and Defence Minister Israel Katz said in a joint statement. The tunnel was part of the Lebanese militia's "terrorist infrastructure" near the village of Majdal Zoun, the statement released on Sunday said.

item.title: From the rubble in Venezuela, an unexpected story of survival cuts through days of tragedy
item.description: Teams gently carried a man from the structure where he had been trapped for four days.

item.title: Searching for Cambodia's stolen crown jewels
item.description: Crowns and precious jewels believed to have been worn by ancient Khmer royalty were tracked down to an unlikely place: a pub parking lot outside London.

item.title: Video shows crocodile that killed Puerto Vallarta man in front of California couple — as new details emerge
item.description: Horrific new video shows the massive crocodile that killed a 28-year-old man in Puerto Vallarta in front of a California couple. Grainy nighttime footage captured the huge reptile with its...

item.title: Pakistan says its security forces killed 29 militants along Afghan border
item.description: Pakistani security forces Sunday carried out an intelligence-based ground operation along the Pakistan-Afghanistan​ border, followed by "calibrated strikes."

item.title: More Than $24 Million Worth of Cocaine Found Hidden Inside Frozen Berry Shipment
item.description: The drugs weighed more than 240 lbs.

item.title: Bear attacks woman after her off-leash dog chases its cub in British Columbia
item.description: Authorities said the bear was reacting defensively rather than attacking without cause.

item.title: Putin acknowledges ‘difficult period’ as Ukraine ramps up attacks
item.description: Kyiv struck a Russian defense plant and set fire to a major oil refinery as part of its efforts to weaken Moscow’s wartime economy.

item.title: South Africa braces for wave of anti-migrant violence
item.description: Thousands of migrants are fleeing South Africa as the country braces for violence after an anti-immigrant group set a Tuesday deadline for all undocumented migrants to leave.

item.title: Heat wave grips Europe, reigniting debate over AC
item.description: Austria, the Czech ​Republic, Germany, and Poland sweat through record temperatures on Sunday, as a European heat wave reignited a political debate over the continent’s preparedness for hotter summers.

item.title: Plane crash in Beijing reveals airspace security gaps
item.description: A small plane crash in China’s capital exposed major gaps in the country’s airspace controls, analysts said, as the government tried to limit public discussion of the incident.

item.title: US and Iran test truce with fresh strikes
item.description: Tehran attacked American military sites in Kuwait and Bahrain after the US accused Iran of attacking a cargo ship in the Strait of Hormuz and targeted Iranian infrastructure in response.

item.title: Putin acknowledges fuel shortages after Ukraine strikes
item.description: Russian President Vladimir Putin acknowledged that the country was suffering from "a certain shortage" of fuel in an interview published by the Kremlin Sunday, after repeated Ukrainian strikes in their four-year war.This part of his interview with Russian journalist Pavel Zarubin was not published by the Kremlin but cited by Russian news agencies.

item.title: Putin interview shows he believes Russia can defeat Ukraine
item.description: Despite clear setbacks for his troops on the front lines in Ukraine, Russian President Vladimir Putin continues to believe that his invading army can defeat Ukraine, according to an interview with Russian state television broadcast on Sunday. The Russian army would continue to do everything to implement the goals of the special operation, as Russia's war against Ukraine is officially called in Moscow, Putin stressed in an interview with Pavel Zarubin, a correspondent for the station.

item.title: From the rubble in Venezuela, an unexpected story of survival cuts through days of tragedy
item.description: A mix of rescue teams and civilians on Sunday peered at a mountain of rubble almost indistinguishable from the other ruins lining swathes of the northern Venezuelan coast following two devastating earthquakes. A pair of dust-coated legs was pulled out of a hole by rescue teams from Virginia, France and Venezuela. “Slow, slow, gentle, gentle,” chanted rescue teams in a mix of Spanish and English, as they passed the father and son through a crowd of onlookers into a nearby ambulance.

The military did not say how the strikes were carried out, but Pakistan's air force has been involved in the previous targeting of alleged militant hideouts inside Afghanistan. In a post on X, Information Minister Attaullah Tarar said the operation was launched in response to multiple militant attacks across the country.

item.title: Thousands demonstrate in Serbian city against President Vučić
item.description: Students and citizens demonstrated on Sunday in the central Serbian city of Kraljevo against the government of Serbian President Aleksandar Vučić. The government reported 1,450 dead from the quakes Sunday afternoon as it faced growing criticism from Venezuelans that its response was inadequate and was overshadowed by civilian-led efforts to rescue people buried under collapsed buildings. Aid agencies consider the first 48 to 72 hours after a natural disaster as crucial for retrieving people alive, though that can be extended if they have access to food and water.

item.title: Ukraine's drone assault ignites major oil refinery in Russia
item.description: Ukraine kept up its heavy drone assault on Russia, setting fire to a major oil refinery in the south and killing at least two people, Russian authorities said Sunday. Ukraine has markedly stepped up its long-range attacks on Russian military industries and energy facilities in recent months, aiming to cut Moscow’s revenue for its invasion — now in its fifth year — and make Russians feel the consequences. “Tonight, our ‘long-range sanctions’ reached two oil refineries in Russia,” Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy wrote on the Telegram messaging app on Sunday.

item.title: Pakistan's military says 3 soldiers were killed in militant attack in Karachi and vows retaliation
item.description: Pakistan’s military said Sunday that three members of its paramilitary Rangers force were killed and four others wounded in fighting after militants launched an attack the previous night on a regional headquarters in the southern port city of Karachi. A militant rammed an explosives-laden vehicle into the provincial headquarters of the paramilitary Pakistan Rangers in Karachi on Saturday night, triggering an intense exchange of gunfire with security forces, according to police and witnesses. In a statement, the military said security forces had killed three attackers and captured a fourth, who was wounded.

item.title: Ugandan army chief orders the closure of a major news platform
item.description: The offices of a major news organization in Uganda were shut down on the orders of the military chief, who warned Sunday that all media “will follow the rules" while asserting his authority as the East African country's de facto ruler. Gen. Muhoozi Kainerugaba, the eldest son of President Yoweri Museveni, has served as the top military commander since 2024. In recent days, after Museveni was sworn in for a seventh consecutive term, Kainerugaba has strengthened his grip with a series of directives and orders usually reserved for the head of state.

item.title: Iraq arrests officials, lawmakers in anti-corruption swoop

Every evening for weeks, protesters have marched by the thousands in the capital Tirana, holding up cut-outs of flamingos.

item.title: The Kent estate at the centre of British diplomacy
item.description: Chevening House near Sevenoaks is a centre of UK soft power as a residence of the foreign secretary.

item.title: Argentinian Cabinet chief resigns amid corruption allegations
item.description: Argentinian President Javier Milei's Cabinet chief, Manuel Adorni, has resigned after months of corruption allegations and an investigation into suspected illicit enrichment. "I have been treated as a criminal and as corrupt, even though not a single act of corruption can be attributed to me," Adorni wrote in a resignation letter.

item.title: Between English and mother tongue: Kenya’s education language dilemma
item.description: Students in Kenya say mother tongue improves learning, but English still dominates education and work.

item.title: Iran attacks Bahrain and Kuwait following US strikes and threatens to halt talks to end the war
item.description: Iran's paramilitary Revolutionary Guard launched drone and missile attacks Sunday targeting Bahrain and Kuwait in response to U.S. airstrikes that hit the Islamic Republic, and threatened a “complete halt” could come to negotiations to end the war if Washington continues its attacks. Efforts to reopen the Strait of Hormuz without Iran's direct oversight sparked the crossfire now gripping the region. A multinational maritime body overseen by the U.S. Navy said Saturday that it would expand a route near Oman to allow for both inbound and outbound traffic, setting up a new flashpoint with Tehran.

item.title: Where are they? Dogs disappear before South Korea meat ban
item.description: Electric prods rest against the wall near rusty cages containing dog skulls in an abandoned canine slaughterhouse in a South Korean town.Only about two dozen were adopted domestically, partly due to South Koreans' preference for small breeds better suited to apartment living.

item.title: Israel’s military and tech industry race to counter Hezbollah’s latest threat
item.description: Fiber-optic drones have become a key weapon for Lebanese militant group Hezbollah in recent months. Israel’s military and tech firms are scrambling to combat them.

item.title: Peruvians say they were promised jobs in Russia, but landed on the front lines in Ukraine
item.description: As Russia’s war in Ukraine grinds on, the Russian military has gone to significant lengths to pad its ranks, including by recruiting foreign fighters from developing countries with promises of high salaries and bonuses.

item.title: Haifa drug lab busted as police crack down on networks flooding the market with illegal substances
item.description: During the operation, officers located an apartment suspected of being used for drug cultivation. A search of the premises led to the seizure of cannabis with a total weight of about seven kilograms.

item.title: American rescue teams pull infant alive from rubble in Venezuela days after devastating twin earthquakes
item.description: Video shows U.S. search-and-rescue teams pulling an infant alive from rubble in Venezuela more than 72 hours after devastating twin earthquakes.

item.title: A painful wait by a pile of rubble in quake-hit Venezuela
item.description: Rescue crews working at one of the many buildings destroyed in Venezuela's killer earthquakes called for silence as they heard some kind of sound from a survivor buried in the rubble.Crews worked into the night, and all day Saturday, in vain.

item.title: Australia boosts shark-spotting drone coverage at Sydney beaches
item.description: Australia will expand shark-spotting drone coverage year-round at beaches across Sydney and beyond from July 1, authorities said Sunday, following a rise in attacks and sightings.Under the expanded drone watch run by the state's lifesavers, about 70 beaches in New South Wales -- including 38 in Sydney -- will be monitored every day.

item.title: I hear my son crying beneath the rubble, says Venezuela earthquake survivor
item.description: Andreina Valerio says she fears for her son, who is trapped with her partner and in-laws beneath a collapsed building.

item.title: Iran's Revolutionary Guards say they hit US targets in Kuwait, Bahrain
item.description: Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) said it had attacked targets in the US-allied Gulf states of Kuwait and Bahrain. In a statement carried by its Sepah News Telegram channel, the IRGC said its navy and air force had jointly launched missile and drone strikes in the early hours of Sunday, destroying eight key US military infrastructure targets in the two countries.

item.title: Kuwait says air defences intercept missiles as Bahrain sounds sirens
item.description: Explosions were reported in the US-allied Gulf states of Kuwait and Bahrain early on Sunday following fresh US strikes on targets in Iran. Kuwait's military said on X that its air defence systems were intercepting hostile missiles and drones.

item.title: Explosions reported as Kiev comes under renewed attack
item.description: Kiev came under renewed attack overnight, with several explosions heard in the Ukrainian capital, the Kyiv Independent reported early on Sunday. Tymur Tkachenko, head of Kiev's military administration, said at least one person was injured in the Darnytsia district of the city The Ukrainian Air Force had earlier warned on Telegram of a ballistic missile threat from the north.

item.title: Several die in swimming accidents as heatwave grips Germany
item.description: At least five people have died in swimming accidents in Germany since Friday, police said, as a heatwave sent many people to lakes and rivers to cool off. Police said a 27-year-old man drowned in the Neckar River near the south-western city of Heidelberg on Saturday, while a 30-year-old man died in a lake near Mannheim.

item.title: UK land-use data shows Britain is far greener than many people think, but for the wrong reasons
item.description: "We’re using a major share of our land to fill a minor part of our diets."

item.title: Great white shark spotted near surfers shuts Sydney's Bondi Beach again
item.description: "As you'll see in this clip, I tried to alert a few people using my flashing drone light."

item.title: Panic in crowded neighborhood as 12-foot king cobra appears near bank before rescuers return it to forest
item.description: "Most incidents occur when the animals act in self-defence."

item.title: Nation of Islam Leader Louis Farrakhan's Wife, Khadijah, Dead at 90
item.description: Louis Farrakhan's wife of more than 70 years has passed away ... the Nation of Islam announced Saturday. The Honorable Minister @LouisFarrakhan with deep sadness yet with profound gratitude to Allah informs you that his beloved wife of 72 years, the First Lady of the Nation of Islam, Mother Khadijah has returned to Allah (may Allah be pleased). The religious organization released a statement ... beginning with a verse from the Quran before stating Mother Khadijah Farrakhan -- the Nation of Islam's First Lady -- has "returned to Allah."

item.title: What news reports from 1600s tell us about life in Mughal India
item.description: Thousands of forgotten news reports are reshaping our understanding of Aurangzeb and the Mughal world.

item.title: Venezuela’s Earthquake: Neighbors Did What The State Could Not
item.description: After Venezuela's earthquake, neighbors rescued the trapped by hand while the state lagged—a reminder that public preparedness, more than solidarity, decides who lives.

item.title: 12-year-old boy is stable after shark attack while swimming with brother in Bahamas
item.description: The database lists 30 confirmed attacks in the Bahamas over the past 400 years.

item.title: VA Task Force 1 conducts rescue missions in Venezuela following earthquakes
item.description: Virginia Task Force 1 Urban Search and Rescue team is conducting around the clock operations in Venezuela to rescue potential survivors of the recent earthquakes, and has been joined by Portsmouth based Mercy Chefs and Virginia Beach based Operation Blessing.

item.title: Why Kim Jong Un never talks about his mother - or her controversial bloodline
item.description: Very few North Koreans know about her, as her origin could threaten the regime’s legitimacy.

item.title: Austrian climber dies in fall in southern Germany
item.description: An Austrian climber died after falling in the Berchtesgaden Alps in southern Germany, police said on Saturday. The 59-year-old had been attempting the "Alte Westwand" climbing route on the Hoher Göll alone, according to police.

item.title: Indian toddler bites cobra he thought was a toy, killing the snake, before collapsing
item.description: The bite was so forceful that the snake was torn into two pieces.

item.title: Australia's NSW rolls out $15,000 interest-free loans for solar, batteries, and home upgrades
item.description: "This program is a massive win for households looking to take control of their energy bills."

item.title: American recounts quake survival as Venezuela death toll climbs
item.description: Sam Brown-Perez said she stayed behind to help people who had been displaced by the earthquakes.

item.title: Australia to double maximum penalty for platforms in breach of social media ban
item.description: Children under the age of 16 have been prevented from 10 key social media platforms in Australia since December 2025.

item.title: How to Help Victims of the Venezuela Earthquake Right Now
item.description: Venezuela was hit by two successive earthquakes, with magnitudes of 7.2 and 7.5. Here's a list of organizations for aid.

item.title: Three Pakistani rangers killed in Karachi attack, Dawn newspaper reports
item.description: KARACHI, June 27 (Reuters) - At least three of Pakistan's Sindh Rangers personnel were killed in an attack on the paramilitary forces building in Karachi, the Dawn newspaper reported on Saturday.

item.title: Solidarity, sadness among Venezuelans made destitute by quake
item.description: Bandaged, bruised Venezuelans sifted through donated clothes in a large sports arena turned shelter after losing everything in the massive earthquakes that struck this week.La Guaira's Pariata Peripheral Hospital -- which has received most of the injured -- treated more than 400 people in the 48 hours following the disaster, a nurse told AFP. The hospital is referring treated patients with nowhere to go towards the sports arena turned shelter.

item.title: Aid planes landing at partially reopened Venezuela airport after quakes
item.description: Venezuela's Simon Bolivar International Airport was partially reopened Saturday and accepting cargo flights carrying aid including a field hospital, the US State Department's top foreign assistance official said."As we got the airport open, we have mobile hospitals coming in today, they're going to be able to hopefully land at Simon Bolivar."

item.title: IDF killed Walid Haniyeh, nephew of former Hamas leader Ismail Haniyeh, military says
item.description: Haniyeh was the deputy commander of a Nukhba company, which was in charge of the infiltration to Israel during the October 7 attacks. The IDF killed Walid Haniyeh, nephew of the former Hamas leader Ismail Haniyeh, during a Thursday operation in Gaza, the military said on Saturday.

item.title: 'We need help': Venezuelans furious at slow official response to quakes
item.description: Boos followed Venezuela's interim leader Delcy Rodriguez during a visit to a Caracas neighborhood pulverized by two massive earthquakes.they shouted at Rodriguez.

item.title: The end of Putin’s regime will spring from war spending chaos, former central bank advisor says, amid military mutiny threat and fuel-shortage brawls
item.description: "It’s less dramatic than a palace coup, but this is what decline looks like."

item.title: See it: Villa containing ruins from Roman Empire uncovered in Egypt
item.description: Archaeologists working with the Supreme Council of Antiquities in Egypt have recently discovered the remains of a Roman residential villa with styles dating back to the Ptolemaic, Roman and Byzantine eras, according to the Ministry of Tourism and Antiquities.

item.title: Gulf countries strongly condemn Iran's drone attack on Bahrain as rising tensions threaten MOU
item.description: Persian Gulf countries are furious over Iran's aggression on Saturday with its drone strikes against targets in Bahrain. This is a major escalation.

item.title: Smell of death hangs over areas devastated by Venezuela quakes as rescuers race to find survivors
item.description: Families are rushing to collapsed buildings where they think their loved ones could be trapped but face an impossible task of trying to move heavy debris.

item.title: As survivors are pulled from rubble of Venezuela earthquakes, relatives of missing hold out hope
item.description: A bloody-faced Daniel Cordero emerged from the rubble of a fallen building in Catia La Mar, Venezuela, surrounded by rescue workers who helped him onto a stretcher as bystanders filmed the scene. Three days after two earthquakes slammed Venezuela, Cordero's rescue, which came Friday, and that of others are providing a jolt of optimism for those still hoping to find their loved ones among the ruins. It comes as the death toll rises from Wednesday’s 7.2 and 7.5 magnitude earthquakes and as many families confirm that mothers, fathers, siblings and children did not survive.

item.title: Venezuela quake death toll rises to 1,430 as rescue window narrows
item.description: Three days after twin earthquakes struck Venezuela, the scale of the disaster is becoming increasingly clear, with the death toll rising to 1,430 and more than 3,200 people injured, authorities said on Saturday. Thousands are still believed to be trapped beneath the rubble, according to National Assembly President Jorge Rodríguez, who said hopes of finding survivors are fading with each passing hour, although rescue workers continue to pull some people out alive.

item.title: Serbia's populist President Vucic says he will resign within weeks as election nears
item.description: Serbia 's populist President Aleksandar Vucic said Saturday he will resign his post within weeks, paving the way for early elections following youth-led protests that shook his tight grip on power. Vucic did not specify exactly when he would resign or when an election, either for Parliament or for a new president, could be held. Vucic, who is currently serving his second term, cannot run again for president, according to Serbia's election law.

item.title: U.S. strikes Iran drone sites; Iran hits Bahrain
item.description: The United States attacked Iranian drone sites Saturday morning, and Iran hit Bahrain in response.

item.title: A Teen Girl's Body Was Found in a Suitcase. Police Arrested a Man Allegedly Seen on Camera with Her, Carrying Similar Luggage
item.description: Police in Thailand allege 17-year-old Tunchanok Donhomla spent her final moments at a hotel with Simon Carman, who denies the charges

item.title: Vancouver turns its Science World museum into a giant 17-story soccer ball for the World Cup
item.description: It started with a whimsical idea. Why not transform the landmark Science World museum in downtown Vancouver, the geodesic dome-topped building not far from BC Place stadium, into a gigantic 17-story soccer ball for the World Cup?

item.description: Hezbollah has condemned a US-brokered agreement to end the war in Lebanon, declaring the deal “null and void”.

item.description: Multiple organizations open crypto donations for Venezuela after the earthquakes. Learn where and how to help.

item.title: Mayor: Russian air defences intercept 21 drones headed to Moscow
item.description: Russian air defences shot down at least 21 Ukrainian drones headed for Moscow over the course of Saturday, Mayor Sergei Sobyanin said. "Experts are currently examining the wreckage," the state news agency TASS quoted him as saying.

item.description: Both the U.S. and Iran have blamed each other for violating the ceasefire memorandum signed last week.

item.title: Forget the ceasefire — The U.S. and Iran are still exchanging attacks over the Strait of Hormuz as Tehran tries to shut down a competing route
item.description: The U.S. carved out an alternate path that runs along Oman's coast and said Saturday that it's been expanded to accommodate inbound and outbound traffic.

item.title: Rescue teams found remains of missing girl swept by Jordan River, search for second one continues
item.description: Authorities are searching for two girls who were reportedly swept away in the Jordan River near Lehavot HaBashan after friends alerted emergency services. The remains of one of the missing girls swept away by the Jordan River near Lahavot HaBashan were found, Magen David Adom reported on Saturday, while authorities continue to search for the second one.

item.title: Hamas deploys armed guards, confiscates phones to suppress Gaza protest plans
item.description: Hamas reportedly intimidated activists, deployed armed operatives, and restricted movement to prevent planned anti-Hamas demonstrations across the Gaza Strip. Mass civilian protests planned across the Gaza Strip against Hamas leadership on Friday were subdued by threats and intimidation from the terror group, according to Hebrew media reports.

item.title: Death toll from Venezuela earthquakes climbs to more than 1,400
item.description: The death toll from earthquakes in Venezuela has risen to 1,430 and more than 3,200 people are injured, President of the National Assembly Jorge Rodríguez said on Saturday. There have been 430 aftershocks following the 7.

item.description: Israel and Lebanon have created a framework for a cease-fire, though Hezbollah is rejecting it because it calls for disarming the organization.

item.description: The strike comes a day after Israel and the Lebanese government signed a US-brokered framework agreement.

item.title: Europe send search and rescue personnel to Venezuela in response to massive quakes
item.description: The European Union has begun sending aid to Venezuela through its Civil Protection Mechanism in response to Wednesday's earthquakes of 7.2 and 7.5 magnitude, which have left more than 50,000 people missing, according to the UN.

item.title: Gunmen attack farming community, killing at least 15 in northwest Nigeria in latest deadly assault
item.description: Gunmen attacked a farming community in a part of northwestern Nigeria that has been no stranger to violence killing at least 15 people, an official said Saturday. The assault took place Friday in the Talata Mafara area of conflict-battered Zamfara state. Abdullaziz Yari, a lawmaker representing the district at the national level, described the assault on the community as a “terrorist attack” in a statement on social media.

item.title: Pilot Dead, 13 People Injured After Plane Crashes Into Beijing’s Tallest Building

item.description: Iran is suspected to have fired on a tanker crossing the Strait of Hormuz in a renewed burst of fighting over the critical waterway.

item.title: The Archbishop of Canterbury is blind to the true enemies of Middle East Christians
item.description: Which country offers the strongest legal protections and lowest risk of persecution for Christians in the Middle East? Where do believers enjoy the greatest safety and the richest opportunities to flourish? The answer, of course, is Israel.

item.description: A woman had a terrifyingly close encounter with a grizzly bear while walking her dog at a wilderness camp in Alberta, Canada.

item.description: Source: Reuters

item.title: Tens of thousands join Munich Pride parade despite sweltering heat
item.description: Tens of thousands of people celebrated Munich's annual Christopher Street Day (CSD) parade on Saturday despite sizzling temperatures across Germany. Christopher Street Day, the term widely used in Germany for Pride celebrations, takes its name from the New York street where the 1969 Stonewall uprising became a defining moment in the modern LGBTQ+ rights movement.

item.title: Up, Up and Away — to the Grave? Iran Floats Flying Funeral for Supreme Leader Ayatollah Khamenei
item.description: Iranian authorities are contemplating holding an "airborne funeral procession" for Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, according to a Saturday report.

item.description: People in central London expressed frustration, uncertainty and cautious hope after Keir Starmer announced his resignation as UK prime minister.

item.title: German autobahn crumbling as heat damage spreads
item.description: Extreme heat has caused growing damage to one of Germany's main east-west motorways, forcing additional closures and lengthy traffic delays as temperatures continued to soar across the country. The affected section of the A2 motorway, which connects Berlin with western Germany, is steadily expanding near the town of Ziesar, according to police.

item.description: Israeli drones struck tents in al-Mawasi, Khan Younis, killing two Palestinians and wounding several others.

item.description: Hezbollah supporters and many others in Lebanon view latest agreement with Israel as a ‘surrender of sovereignty’.

item.description: By Gergely Szakacs and Fedja Grulovic BUDAPEST, June 27 (Reuters) - More than 10,000 Hungarians on Saturday joined Budapest's first annual Pride march since right-wing leader Viktor Orban's election

item.title: Lebanon's deal with Israel requires Hezbollah to disarm. That might be difficult
item.description: Hezbollah’s leader on Saturday criticized a framework agreement that Israel and Lebanon signed a day earlier to end months of conflict between the militant group and Israel, raising concerns about its effectiveness. Lebanon and Israel signed the deal in Washington on Friday without Hezbollah. The agreement links Israel’s withdrawal from Lebanon to the Iran-backed militant group’s disarmament, something Hezbollah rejects.

item.title: Tens of thousands march in the first Budapest Pride since Viktor Orbán was voted out
item.description: Tens of thousands of people gathered in soaring temperatures in Hungary's capital on Saturday to celebrate the 31st annual Budapest Pride, the first such LGBTQ+ march since former Prime Minister Viktor Orbán, who had sought to ban the event, was ousted in an April election. Participants set off from Budapest's iconic Opera house and wound through the city center before crossing the Erzsébet Bridge over the Danube River. Luca Új, who was participating in her third Pride event, said she felt the mood at the march was more relaxed now that Orbán's government, which implemented numerous anti-LGBTQ+ policies during its 16 years in power, had been defeated.

item.title: Authorities acknowledge Beijing plane crash; pilot dead, 13 hurt
item.description: The local authorities acknowledged the plane crash that hit Beijing's tallest building Friday, saying the pilot died and 13 people were injured.

item.description: The new Persian Gulf strikes show the danger of the war again spinning out of control.

item.title: Hezbollah chief calls Israel-Lebanon deal a 'humiliation'
item.description: Hezbollah leader Naim Qassem on Saturday denounced the agreement between Israel and Lebanon as a "humiliation," a "disgrace" and a surrender of Lebanese sovereignty, calling for the deal to be scrapped. Addressing his remarks to the Lebanese government, Qassem said it was time to reverse what he described as mistaken decisions that would otherwise lead the country to ruin, according to a statement carried by Hezbollah's Al-Manar television channel.

item.title: Israeli drone strike kills Palestinian siblings in a Gaza tent camp
item.description: DEIR AL-BALAH, Gaza Strip (AP) — An Israeli drone strike on Saturday killed two Palestinian siblings, including a 15-year-old girl, in southern Gaza and wounded at least seven others, according to Nasser hospital, where the casualties were taken. The strike targeted tents sheltering displaced Palestinians in the sprawling camp of Muwasi, killing 15-year-old Islam Moussa and her 30-year-old brother, Abdullah Moussa. The Israeli military acknowledged it had struck the area of Muwasi, saying it had targeted a Hamas militant but did not immediately provide more information.

item.title: Jerusalem police arrest caregiver on suspicion of abuse and neglect of toddlers
item.description: The complaint alleges the caregiver "shouted and cursed at the toddlers, and in some cases even ignored their crying and needs while they were in her care. " A 49-year-old caregiver from the Har Homa neighborhood of Jerusalem was arrested on Wednesday on suspicion of abusing and neglecting two toddlers under their care, the Israel Police Spokesperson's unit said in a statement on Thursday.

item.title: Chinese dissident who fled by dinghy to South Korea arrives in Canada, his friend says
item.description: A Chinese political dissident who had fled to South Korea last month in a dinghy has arrived in Canada, his friend said on social media on Saturday. Dong Guangping was aboard a 3.3-meter (10.8-foot) inflatable boat in the waters off a western South Korean island in May when he was detained by South Korea’s coast guard for allegedly violating the country’s immigration law. It was his fourth known attempt to flee China.

item.title: Tourist traffic protest closes another Austrian mountain road
item.description: A month after protesters shut down the Alpine Brenner Pass, the Fernpass mountain road in the Austrian state of Tyrol was closed on Saturday for a further demonstration against high tourist traffic in the region. Chaos on the B179 was avoided, however, according to the Austrian Automobile Club (ÖAMTC), which was monitoring the situation.

item.title: Kyiv and Moscow trade strikes as Ukraine hits arms factory deep in Russia
item.description: Ukraine said it used its FP-5 Flamingo cruise missiles overnight to strike Russia's Titan-Barrikady weapons plant, which is thought to be involved in the production of parts for the Oreshnik missile.

item.title: Former German leader says a female president would be a good sign
item.description: Former German president Joachim Gauck said having a woman as head of state is long overdue in the nation. It is "absolutely appropriate for a woman to hold the office for a very practical reason: When we see how many areas of public life are supported by women, a female federal president would simply send a logical signal," he told the Sunday edition of the conservative German daily Die Welt.

item.description: A MiG-29 fighter plane has crashed in the central Ukrainian region of Poltava, according to an official report on Saturday. The pilot ejected safely and is receiving medical treatment, the Ukrainian Air Force said following the incident during a nighttime combat mission.

item.title: Israel, Lebanon agree to end conflict with Hezbollah but doubts remain

The Lebanese regular army is set to take control there and, under the framework agreement, Israel and Lebanon also commit to ending their decades-long conflict.

item.description: By Samuel McKeith SYDNEY, June 27 (Reuters) - Australia said on Saturday it would double the maximum penalty it can impose on tech firms found to have failed to uphold a ground-breaking social media ban for children, as evidence mounts that the ‌ban has had little effect on teen use. The government will also strengthen the information-gathering powers of its internet regulator, the ‌eSafety Commissioner, allowing it to compel social media companies to provide evidence of what they have done to s

item.title: Australia toughens kids' social media ban, doubles potential penalties for tech firms
item.description: Australia said on Saturday it would double the maximum penalty it can impose on tech firms found to have failed to uphold a ground-breaking social media ban for children, as evidence mounts that the ‌ban has had little effect on teen use. The government will also strengthen the information-gathering powers of its internet regulator, the ‌eSafety Commissioner, allowing it to compel social media companies to provide evidence of what they have done to stop under-16s from getting an account. Under the changes, the maximum penalty ​for systematic failures to uphold the ban jumps to A$99 million ($68 million) from A$49.5 million.

item.title: Ukraine: main oil pumping station for Moscow struck again
item.description: The Ukrainian Security Service (SBU) said on Saturday that it has again used drones to attack a Russian oil pumping station crucial for Moscow's fuel supply. A technical building at the facility in Vtorovo, near the capital of the Vladimir region east of Moscow, was hit in the operation, according to an SBU statement.

item.title: Beijing confirms plane collision with skyscraper in no-fly zone

Ukraine has stepped up its long-range aerial attacks on Russian military industries and energy facilities, aiming to cut Moscow’s revenue for the war and make Russians feel the consequences of the invasion, now in its fifth year.

item.title: A small plane slammed into the tallest skyscraper in China’s capital. Hours later, it was like nothing had happened.

item.title: Iran condemns new US attacks
item.description: The Iranian Foreign Ministry strongly condemned the latest US airstrikes on several targets on the country's southern coast. This is a "blatant violation" of the framework agreement, the ministry said in a statement distributed by the ISNA news agency on Saturday.

item.title: Iranian drones attack Bahrain and a ship is struck in the strait after US airstrikes on Iran
item.description: Iran launched a drone assault targeting Bahrain while a ship in the Strait of Hormuz separately came under attack Saturday, likely Tehran's response to overnight airstrikes by the United States. The attacks across the Persian Gulf show the danger of the Iran war again spinning out of control, even after Iran and the U.S. reached an interim deal to try and agree on a final accord to end the conflict. The U.S. had launched its airstrikes in response to an Iranian drone attack on a ship trying to get out of the strait on Thursday, continuing a string of attacks that have shaken the uneasy ceasefire in the war.

item.title: A small plane has crashed into a Beijing high-rise, killing the pilot and injuring 13
item.description: Chinese authorities on Saturday said a small plane that crashed into a building in Beijing the day before had killed the pilot and injured 13 others. The authorities of the Chaoyang district, a vibrant business area, said a two-seat light sport aircraft collided with a high-rise building near the East Third Ring Road at 5:55 p.m. on Friday and caused the casualties. The short statement on WeChat did not identify the building or the pilot, who the authorities said was the only person on the craft.

item.title: Older buildings and substandard construction left Venezuela vulnerable to earthquakes
item.description: Older buildings, substandard construction and geography left many neighborhoods in Venezuela vulnerable to strong earthquakes like the ones that struck the country this week. Engineers and other experts said the back-to-back earthquakes on Wednesday were among the most intense to hit the country in more than a century, leveling buildings and leaving more than 900 dead with the number expected to rise. Videos and satellite imagery from the disaster zone reviewed by The Associated Press reveal scores of multistory buildings had collapsed.

item.title: Crucial window for rescuing survivors narrows as Venezuela enters third day after deadly twin quakes
item.description: The situation has grown more desperate by the hour in Venezuela as people dig through the rubble of collapsed homes and apartment buildings three days after the devastating one-two punch of 7.2 and 7.5-magnitude earthquakes, knowing time is running out to find survivors. Authorities announced Friday night that they would block access to La Guaira, the epicenter of the destruction, as chaos and traffic began to hamper search efforts. People reported seeing few state rescue teams in the hardest-hit areas, despite authorities projecting an image of a robust government response.

With their long and crescent-shaped horns, the Inyambo cows graze at the King’s Palace Museum in Nyanza and at the homes of some of Rwanda's richest people, including President Paul Kagame. The breed is descended from the distinctive Ankole cattle of East Africa and is also found in neighboring Uganda and Burundi.

item.title: Congo files an ICJ case against Rwanda over decades of violence in eastern Congo
item.description: Congo said Friday it has filed a case against Rwanda at the International Court of Justice, accusing its neighbor of bearing legal responsibility for more than three decades of violence that has devastated eastern Congo. Congo accused Rwanda of breaching international conventions on genocide, racial discrimination, discrimination against women and torture. It said civilians in the east have suffered massacres, extrajudicial killings, torture, sexual violence, forced displacement and ethnic and gender-based discrimination since the 1990s.

item.description: The charity Doctors Without Borders (MSF) has reported an alarming increase in the number of malnourished children in southern Afghanistan. item.description: Japanese World Cup fans once again drew international praise this week after supporters were seen cleaning up stadium stands following matches. Within hours, a viral social media post challenged the celebration, arguing that Japanese men spend the least time on housework among developed nations. The post drew nearly 2 million views and ignited debate over unpaid labor, childcare and gender roles inside Japanese households.

item.title: Nepal becomes 1st country in South Asia to legalize same-sex marriage
item.description: Nepal’s Supreme Court has issued a final ruling affirming marriage equality as a constitutional right, making the Himalayan nation the first in South Asia to legally recognize same-sex unions, and the third in Asia after Taiwan and Thailand. The ruling is the latest in a series of decisions, most recently a 2023 interim order triggered when nine LGBTQ+ activists sued over marriage statutes that restricted unions to men and women. As we have reported, that interim order led to the country’s first same-sex marriage registration in November 2023, when Surendra Pandey and Maya Gurung, who had been together nine years, formalized their union at the municipal office of Dordi in Lamjung district.

The hotel operator was sentenced to 13 years and four months in prison.

item.title: Sergei Ivanov, Russia's ex-defense minister once seen as Putin's likely successor, has died at 73
item.description: Sergei Ivanov, a former Russian defense minister once seen as a possible successor to President Vladimir Putin, has died. The Kremlin said Ivanov died on Friday without providing the cause of death or giving other details. Putin sent his condolences to Ivanov's family.

item.title: Missile alert goes off in Dubai but authorities quickly tell the public to disregard the warning
item.description: Authorities in the United Arab Emirates sounded an incoming missile alert on Friday for Dubai before quickly telling the public to “disregard the previous warning,” an indication it was likely triggered by mistake. The mobile phone alert startled many across Dubai, the first since the United States and Iran last week reached an interim ceasefire in the war. The warning underscored the ongoing tensions in the region, following an attack on a cargo vessel in the Strait of Hormuz and Israeli strikes in Lebanon over the past few days.

item.description: The authorities appointed by Moscow on the Black Sea peninsula of Crimea, which Russia annexed in 2014, have declared a regional state of emergency. The move was announced on Friday by the appointed head of Crimea, Sergei Aksyonov, and the appointed governor of Sevastopol, Mikhail Razvozhayev.

item.title: Thousands mark Ashura in Lebanon amid tensions with Israel
item.description: Tens of thousands of people in Lebanon gathered on Friday for mourning ceremonies marking Ashura, the annual Shiite commemoration of the martyrdom of Imam Hussein, the grandson of the prophet Mohammed, against the backdrop of severe tensions with Israel. One of the largest Ashura processions in recent years took place along Beirut's old airport road, outside the southern suburbs.

item.title: Europol identifies 731 organized crime networks in Europe
item.description: A Europol investigation has revealed that 731 criminal networks are active in Europe, involving more than 400,000 members of 118 different nationalities, the European police agency said on Friday. The Europol study says gangs often operate across borders and in a highly professional manner, with 85% of them using legal business structures to carry out their crimes.

item.title: Ministry slams German school's brothel assignment
item.description: A classroom assignment involving the design of a fictional brothel as part of a sex education lesson at a grammar school in Germany's Lower Rhine region has been criticized by the state's Ministry of Education as inappropriate. According to media reports from Friday, the Year 8 pupils in the town of Kevelaer in the state of North Rhine-Westphalia were asked to work in groups to design a brothel entitled "New Brothel for Everyone.

item.title: Iran reasserts its right to control shipping in Strait of Hormuz after ship hit near Oman
item.description: DUBAI/LONDON, June 26 (Reuters) - Tehran reasserted its right on Friday to control shipping in the Strait of Hormuz and warned Gulf states against siding with the U., a day after an attack on a ship near Oman highlighted the fragility of a preliminary deal to end the Iran war.

item.title: EU unveils plan to help the bloc's overlooked islands and coastal communities
item.description: Improving transportation links, ending energy isolation and building more homes are some of the key initiatives the European Union’s executive arm is looking to enact under a pair of first-ever strategies designed to help the bloc’s islands and coastal communities flourish, EU officials said Friday. “Islands have been overlooked in the past, but now that comes to an end,” European Parliament Vice President Younous Omarjee told a conference on strengthening EU islands and coastal communities.

item.title: Russia-annexed Crimea declares 'emergency' amid Ukraine strikes
item.description: Authorities in Russian-annexed Crimea on Friday declared an "emergency situation" in a bid to ease the fallout from increasing Ukrainian aerial attacks on the peninsula.Russian air defences shot down 660 Ukrainian drones overnight, including over the capital Moscow and the annexed Crimea, its defence ministry said Friday, one of the highest figures since the start of the conflict.
